coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-decorated Na2W4O13 composite with WO3 for highly efficient photocatalytic antibacterial activity
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Jingtao
Liu, Xing
Wang, Xueying
Mu, Lilong
Yuan, Mingming
Liu, Bingkun
Shi, Hengzhen
coredata.dc:description: Photodisinfection by semiconductors has been proven to be an effective method for achieving antibacterial or antifungal activity. However, the toxicity of the nanomaterial to the environment and organisms is a major concern. Herein, a highly efficient and environmentally friendly photodisinfection material of a carbon dots (CDs) decorated Na2W4O13 composite with WO3 photocatalyst was fabricated via a facile hydrothermal-calcination approach. The TEM (transmission electron microscopy) images showed that CDs decorated the surface of the Na2W4O13 flakes. Compared with the samples without incorporated CDs, the as-synthesized composite of CDs/Na2W4O13/WO3 exhibited excellent antimicrobial activity against E. coli under visible light illumination. Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy and reactive species scavenging experiments revealed that the hydroxyl radicals and superoxide radical anions played the most important role in the photocatalytic bacterial inactivation. Furthermore, the cytotoxicity of the CDs/Na2W4O13/WO3 composite was evaluated by analyzing the viability of HepG2 and Chinese hamster lung (V79) cells using Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Composite
Photocatalysis
Antibacterial
CCK-8

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of WO3, Na2W4O13, WO3/Na2W4O13 composite and CDs/WO3/Na2W4O13 composite calcined at different temperature.
(a) and (b) TEM images of the CDs/WO3/Na2W4O13 composite at different magnifications. (c) and (d) HRTEM images of the CDs from (b). (e) and (f) HRTEM images.
(a) FTIR spectra of WO3, Na2W4O13, WO3/Na2W4O13 composite and the CDs/WO3/Na2W4O13 composite. (b) High-resolution C1 s XPS spectrum.
(a) and (b) Optical absorbance and PL spectra of WO3, Na2W4O13, WO3/Na2W4O13 composite and the CDs/WO3/Na2W4O13 composite.
Photocatalytic disinfection of E. coli by WO3, Na2W4O13, WO3/Na2W4O13 composite and the CDs/WO3/Na2W4O13 composite under visible light (λ > 400 nm) irradiation.
SEM images of the E. coli cell untreated or treated with CDs/WO3/Na2W4O13 composite under visible light irradiation.

Photocatalytic inactivation efficiency of E. coli by the CDs/WO3/Na2W4O13 composite with different scavengers (0.1 mM potassium dichromate, 0.5 mM sodium oxalate, 1 mM isopropyl alcohol and 10 mM 4-hydroxy-TEMPO).
ESR spectra of the (a) DMPO-O2- and (b) DMPO-OH adducts recorded with the CDs/WO3/Na2W4O13 composite under visible light (λ > 400 nm) irradiation.

Cytotoxicity of the CDs/Na2W4O13/WO3 composite evaluated based on the viability of V79 cells (a) and HepG2 cells (b) by CCK-8 assay. The different letters above the curve illustrate the significant differences between the treatments at p＜0.05. The capital letters indicate significant differences between the various concentrations in each group, while the lowercase letters represent significant differences between treatment times.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-decorated Na2W4O13 composite with WO3 for highly efficient photocatalytic antibacterial activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Collaborative Innovation Centre of Food Production and Safety, Henan Key Laboratory of Cold Chain Food Quality and Safety Control, School of Food and Bioengineering, Zhengzhou University of Light Industry, Zhengzhou, 450002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Collaborative Innovation Centre of Food Production and Safety
Henan Key Laboratory of Cold Chain Food Quality and Safety Control

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material and Chemical Engineering, Zhengzhou University of Light Industry, Zhengzhou, 450002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Material and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photodisinfection by semiconductors has been proven to be an effective method for achieving antibacterial or antifungal activity. However, the toxicity of the nanomaterial to the environment and organisms is a major concern. Herein, a highly efficient and environmentally friendly photodisinfection material of a carbon dots (CDs) decorated Na2W4O13 composite with WO3 photocatalyst was fabricated via a facile hydrothermal-calcination approach. The TEM (transmission electron microscopy) images showed that CDs decorated the surface of the Na2W4O13 flakes. Compared with the samples without incorporated CDs, the as-synthesized composite of CDs/Na2W4O13/WO3 exhibited excellent antimicrobial activity against E. coli under visible light illumination. Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy and reactive species scavenging experiments revealed that the hydroxyl radicals and superoxide radical anions played the most important role in the photocatalytic bacterial inactivation. Furthermore, the cytotoxicity of the CDs/Na2W4O13/WO3 composite was evaluated by analyzing the viability of HepG2 and Chinese hamster lung (V79) cells using Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8).

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, pathogens have exhibited resistance to single or multiple antibiotics due to the extensive release and misuse of antibiotics in recent decades, which has created an increasing global public health threat [1,2]. However, there is a shortage of new antibiotics that can completely solve this problem [3]. Thus, other strategies to protect against pathogenic bacteria are urgently needed. To date, several methods have been developed to replace antibiotics, such as antibacterial nanosilver materials [4], photocatalytic disinfection [5], and photodynamic therapy. Among these methods, photocatalytic disinfection, pioneered by Matsunaga et al. and based on reactive oxygen species (ROS), is the most different from the conventional methods. Compared with widely used disinfection methods, such as antibiotic and chlorine disinfectants, the photocatalytic disinfection of microbial pathogens could reduce the formation of potentially harmful disinfection byproducts (DBPs), effectively disinfect the whole spectrum of pathogens, and utilize solar illumination as the energy input for the disinfection process. Thus, photocatalytic disinfection, an environmentally friendly and sustainable treatment technology, has attracted increasing attention and has been widely studied in recent years due to the advantages described above [6–8].

Tungsten oxide (WO3)-based materials have been shown to be important photocatalysts with advantages of nontoxicity, abundant sources and stability in acidic media. These materials have been implemented in various applications, such as dye-sensitized solar cells, sensors, hydrogen evolution from water splitting, water and air purification, CO2 photoreduction, and bacterial disinfection. Additionally, the experimental band gap energy of WO3 (2.5–2.8 eV) makes it an attractive visible-light-driven photocatalyst [9]. However, two main obstacles inhibit the practical photocatalytic application of pure WO3. One is the relatively low conduction band (CB) edge with respect to the standard H+/H2 redox potential [10,11]. The other significant obstacle is the rapid recombination of photogenerated charge carriers. Therefore, many strategies such as surface hybridization with graphene [12–14], noble metal deposition [15–17], and coupling with other semiconductors [18–21] have been widely studied to improve the photocatalytic activity of WO3. On the one hand, Na2W4O13, which has a band gap of ∼3.1 eV, has been shown to exhibit useful properties such as luminescence, ionic conduction and water splitting [22,23]. Importantly the CB level of Na2W4O13 is more negative than that of WO3 and is suitable not only for O2 production but also for H2 production in photocatalytic water splitting.

Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted broad interests due to their outstanding physical and chemical properties, such as low toxicity, good chemical stability, excellent upconversion photoluminescence (UCPL) behavior, unique photoinduced electron transfer and promising electron reservoir properties [24–28]. The outstanding electronic properties of CDs as electron donors and acceptors make them promising modifiers for catalysis [29,30]. In addition, the upconversion function of CDs allows the visible and near-infrared spectrum of sunlight to be utilized, which can enhance the photocatalytic efficiency of semiconductors [31]. Therefore, CDs can be utilized as modifying materials to enhance the photocatalytic activity of traditional photocatalysts by decreasing electron-hole recombination and broadening the photoabsorption region. Very recently, CDs have been coupled with semiconductors, such as TiO2 [31], C3N4 [25,29], WO3 [32], Cu2O [33] and others [34], to improve their photocatalytic activity.

Here, we introduced a new strategy for the synthesis of a CDs/Na2W4O13/WO3 composite by the use of a palladium-mediated hydrothermal reaction. The photodisinfection properties of the CDs/Na2W4O13/WO3 composite was evaluated by monitoring the survival rate of E. coli under visible light irradiation. Moreover, the dominant reactive species in photodisinfection by the CDs/Na2W4O13/WO3 composite was determined by ESR and RS scavenging experiments. Furthermore, a cell counting experiment demonstrated that the CDs/Na2W4O13/WO3 composite could be used as an environmentally friendly photodisinfection material.

The CDs-decorated and undecorated samples were first synthesized by a solvothermal reaction and subsequently calcined in air according to the previously reported procedure [35] with proper modifications, as briefly described below. First, 6 mmol of WCl6 and 0.006 mmol of PdCl2 (0.1% molar ratio relative toWCl6) were dissolved in 30 mL of ethanol and stirred vigorously for 1 h to obtain mixture A. Second, 30 mL of NaOH (36 mmol) ethanol solution was slowly added dropwise into mixture A at room temperature. Third, the suspension was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, maintained at 200 °C for 6 h, and then cooled to room temperature. After the solvothermal reaction, the obtained precipitate was washed once each with distilled water and ethanol and then dried at 80 °C overnight. After drying, the precipitate was crushed into a fine powder and calcined at varying temperatures of 350 °C, 450 °C and 550 °C in air for 5 h to obtain the sample, and marked as WO3, CDs/Na2W4O13/WO3, Na2W4O13. A reference sample, Na2W4O13/WO3, was prepared in the same way but without the addition of PdCl2 and was calcinated at 450 °C.

The crystal structure was characterized by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) with Cu Kα radiation on a D8 Advance X-ray Diffractometer (Bruker, Germany).The scan range of 2θ was 10° to 80° at a scan rate of 0.5°/min. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were examined by a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were obtained on a JSM-7001 F scanning electron microscope (JEOL) at an acceleration voltage of 10 kV. The presence of C, Na, W and O in the CDs/Na2W4O13/WO3 composite was analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The XPS measurements were made using an ESCALAB250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., MA) with an Al K anode (1486.6 eV photon energy, 300 W). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were recorded on a Nicolet 6700 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc.) to analyze the functional groups in the samples. The optical absorbance was determined from the diffuse reflectance measurements obtained on a U-3900H UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi Limited, Japan) using BaSO4 as the reference. Steady state photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the materials were obtained on a fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi F-7000, Japan) with 385 nm excitation at room temperature.

A fixed concentration of 1 mg photocatalyst/mL E. coli suspension was used in the experiments. A 10 mg portion of the photocatalyst in 9.9 mL of a buffer solution was first injected into a sterile 60 mm × 15 mm Petri dish and dispersed ultrasonically for 10 min. Then, 0.1 mL of the E. coli suspension (ca. 109CFU/mL) was added to the Petri dish to give an initial E. coli concentration of ca. 107CFU/mL in the photocatalytic disinfection experiments.

A 300 W xenon lamp (MicroSolar300, Beijing Perfect Light Technology Co. Ltd., China) was used for the photocatalytic inactivation experiments and as the light source with a UV cut-off filter (λ > 400 nm). The light intensity striking the cells was ca. 10 mW/cm2, as measured by an FZ-A optical radiometer (Photoelectric Instrument Factory of Beijing Normal University, China). A 0.1 mL aliquot of the cell suspension was withdrawn at regular time intervals. Following appropriate dilution with PBS buffer solution (pH 7), the 0.1 mL aliquots were spread onto an agar medium plate and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. Then, the number of viable cells in terms of colony-forming units was counted. Control experiments were also carried out in the dark with the photocatalyst under otherwise identical conditions. For the radical scavenger study, an appropriate concentration of one of the four radical scavengers, potassium dichromate, sodium oxalate, isopropyl alcohol and 4-hydroxy-TEMPO, was added into the reaction solution under identical conditions as those described above. All analyses were conducted in triplicate. The cell culture and SEM observation are given in detail in supplementary information.

Electron spin resonance (ESR) was used to characterize the formation of OH and O2 −, and spectra were recorded on a JES-FA200 (JEOL, Japan) electron spin resonance instrument at room temperature. ESR signals were measured of the radicals spin trapped by DMPO. The involvement of O2 − was examined in methanol due to the instability of O2 − in water, and the involvement of OH was detected in ultrapure water. In the samples, 50 mg of the as-prepared photocatalyst was mixed with 50 mL of a DMPO (100 mM) solution. Spectra were recorded at different time intervals during irradiation by a 300 W xenon lamp with a UV cut-off filter to provide visible light (λ > 400 nm).

To prove the potential disinfection utility of this composite from the viewpoint of safety, we examined the viability of HepG2 andV79 cells by CCK-8 assay at various concentrations of the composite. Cells in the logarithmic phase (100 μL, 104 mL−1) were seeded in a 96-well culture plate and cultured overnight in an incubator at 37 °C in 5% CO2 for sufficient adherence. Various concentrations (0.1, 0.2, 0.4, and 0.8 mg mL−1) of the CDs/Na2W4O13/WO3 composite in DMEM solution were added to the cell plate as the treated group, and the control group was treated with an equal volume of PBS. After 24 or 48 h, the cell culture medium was discarded and replaced with fresh medium without CDs/Na2W4O13/WO3. Then, 10 μL of CCK-8 solution was added to each well of the 96-well plate and incubated at 37 °C for 4 h. The absorbance at 450 nm was then measured using an iMark microplate reader (BIO-RAD, USA). Those tests were carried out under dark conditions and repeated three times. All the results were analyzed using analysis of variance. Statistical significance was considered at p <0.05. After the CCK assay, the cells were imaged by an Observer A1 inverted microscope (Zeiss, Germany).

XRD patterns were collected to investigate the crystalline phase structure of the as-prepared samples. Fig. 1 shows the typical XRD patterns of the prepared samples. After calcination at 450 °C, both the triclinic phase of Na2W4O13 (ICDD/JCPDS PDF﹟27-1425) and the orthorhombic phase of WO3 (ICDD/JCPDS PDF﹟20-1324) were observed in the samples with or without 0.1% PdCl2 added. However, when calcined at 350 °C and 550 °C, the phase was WO3 and Na2W4O13, respectively. No XRD diffraction peaks for PdO or Pd were observed in these conditions.

The morphology and microstructure of the obtained samples were characterized by TEM and SEM. Fig. S1 shows the TEM images of the samples calcined at 350 °C (a and b) and 550 °C (c and d).The WO3 and flake Na2W4O13 phases can be seen in Fig. S1a and S1c.The HRTEM images of WO3 (Fig. S1b) and Na2W4O13 (Fig. S1d) exhibit lattice spacing of 0.369 nm and 0.351 nm, respectively, which are assigned to the in-plane reflection of the (200) lattice plane of WO3 and the (210) lattice plane of Na2W4O13. Figs. 2 and S2 show the TEM images of the samples calcined at 450 °C with (Figs. S2a, S2b and 2) and without (Fig. S2c and S2d) the addition of 0.1% PdCl2. Particles with flakes are observed in Fig. S2a and S2b. In addition, nanoparticles decorate the surface of the flake materials. In contrast, a relatively cleaner surface is observed in Fig. S2c and S2d, as no nanoparticles are visible on the surface of the materials calcined in the absence of PdCl2. As shown in Fig. 2a and b, small particles approximately 6 nm in diameter are clearly observed on the surface of the flake materials. HRTEM images of the materials are provided in Fig. 2c–f; two nanoparticles from Fig. 2b are shown in Fig. 2c and d, and the image shown in 2f is an enlargement of the image in 2e. The d-spacings of the flakes were determined to be ∼0.407, ∼0.26, and ∼0.411 nm, corresponding to the (020), (-211) and (200) planes of Na2W4O13. Thus, the TEM image indicated that the Na2W4O13 phase formed a flake shape. A set of lattice planes with a d-spacing of ∼0.385 nm corresponding to the (001) plane of WO3 could be easily identified on one crystallite in Fig. 2e, which was consistent with the XRD analysis. The HRTEM images of the nanoparticles (in Fig. 2c, d and f) on the surface of the Na2W4O13 flakes reveal a lattice spacing of 0.211 nm, assigned to the in-plane reflection of the (100) lattice plane of graphitic carbon [36,37]. The HRTEM images indicated that the surfaces of the Na2W4O13 flakes were decorated with CDs. Fig. S3 shows SEM images of the CDs/Na2W4O13/WO3 composite with different magnifications, and the flakes with particles on the surface could be observed in these images. However, the formation mechanism of CDs is unclear in these conditions. And, the existence of CDs is further confirmed by FTIR and XPS.

The FTIR spectra of the as-prepared Na2W4O13, CDs/Na2W4O13/WO3, WO3 and Na2W4O13/WO3 materials are shown in Fig. 3 a. Several characteristic peaks are observed at approximately 1045, 1400 and 1636 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum of the CDs/Na2W4O13/WO3 composite and may be ascribed to the typical stretching vibrations of COC, −COO and CC/C = O, respectively [29,38]. The absorption peak at 2845-2975 cm−1 was assigned to C–H vibrations. None of the characteristic peaks of CDs were observed in the FTIR spectrum of Na2W4O13. Although a band was observed at approximately 1636 cm-1 in the spectra of WO3 and Na2W4O13/WO3, there were clearly no CDs in these materials based on the TEM images shown in Figs. S1 and S2. The band located at approximately 3445 cm-1 in the spectra of CDs/Na2W4O13/WO3, WO3 and Na2W4O13/WO3 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of adsorbed H2O [39]. The lack of an obvious vibration peak at this position in the spectrum of Na2W4O13 could be due to the higher calcination temperature used for this material.

The XPS spectra of the CDs/Na2W4O13/WO3 composite are depicted in Figs. 4 b and S4. The survey XPS spectrum shows the presence of the four main elements, W, Na, C and O, on the surface of the CDs/Na2W4O13/WO3 composite. Figs. 4b and S4 show the high-resolution C 1 s, W 4f, Na1 s, and O 1 s XPS spectra. The C 1 s spectrum of CDs/Na2W4O13/WO3 could be divided into three peaks at 284.6, 286.6, and 288.4 eV, which were assigned to C-C, C–O, and C = O, respectively [36,40]. In Fig. S4a, the two peaks at 35.8 and 37.9 eV were assigned to W 4f7/2 and W 4f5/2, respectively, of the W6+species in CDs/Na2W4O13/WO3 [11]. The O1 s spectrum (Fig. S4d) shows that the lattice oxygen in this sample had a binding energy of 530.6 eV for both Na2W4O13 and WO3 [11]. Based on the high-resolution XPS spectra, the atomic percentages of W and Na were 18.34 and 8.41, respectively. Thus, the molar ratio of WO3:Na2W4O13 was calculated to be ∼3:8. No binding energy of Pd 3d peaks were observed in these conditions (Fig. S4c), which could be due to the low content of Pd amount. However, in our previously work, the PdO was found after calcination [35]. Thus, the effect of PdO on the photodisinfection performance was not discussed in this work.

The light-response characteristics of the as-synthesized materials were investigated by UV–vis and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopies. Fig. 4a is the optical absorbance of all the samples. As shown in Fig. 4a, each sample has its own characteristics. Na2W4O13 appreciably absorbed mainly in UV light with wavelengths shorter than 400 nm. WO3 exhibited a characteristic spectrum with a distinct absorption band at ∼475 nm, which agrees with that of a previous report [32]. After the formation of a composite with WO3, Na2W4O13/WO3 showed a red shift in the absorbance of Na2W4O13 into the visible range. The absorption spectrum of the CDs/Na2W4O13/WO3 composite showed a clear red shift in the absorbance relative to that of the Na2W4O13/WO3 composite. This shift could be induced by certain functional groups, such as CC, C–O, and C–H [31]. The PL analysis was used to examine the separation efficiency of the photoinduced electrons and holes. Lower fluorescence emission intensity suggests higher separation efficiency and an enhanced photocatalytic activity. Fig. 4b shows the PL spectra of the Na2W4O13, Na2W4O13/WO3, WO3 and CDs/Na2W4O13/WO3 materials following excitation with 385 nm light. The PL intensity of the CDs/Na2W4O13/WO3 composite was remarkably lower than that of the other materials, suggesting that the introduction of CDs efficiently inhibited the recombination of the photogenerated charge carriers via trapping of the photogenerated electrons.

The photocatalytic performance of the as-synthesized composite for disinfection was evaluated with E. coli (107CFU/mL) under visible light irradiation. Fig. 5 shows the survivor ratios of the E. coli cells following different treatments. Under only visible light illumination, the survival ratio of the E. coli was 91.3%, the death of E. coli could be induced by photodamage of the cell. After visible light irradiation for 100 min, ∼68.3% of the E. coli cells treated with Na2W4O13 survived, which have no more than one-log reduce and this could be attributed to the large band gap of the material. For treatment with the synthesized WO3/Na2W4O13 and WO3 materials, approximately 72.6% and 0.6%, respectively, of the E. coli cells were alive. Thus, the disinfection performance of WO3/Na2W4O13 and WO3 shown about one-log and two-log reduction. However, the CDs-decorated Na2W4O13/WO3 composite showed the best photocatalytic bactericidal activity, with approximately 2 × 107 CFU/mL of the E. coli cells completely inactivated within 100 min, which have seven-log reduction. Those was much faster than the rates exhibited by WO3, Na2W4O13 and Na2W4O13/WO3. The enhanced antibacterial activity of the CDs/Na2W4O13/WO3 composite was related to the PL intensity, this could be due to the excellent upconversion photoluminescence (UCPL) behavior, unique photoinduced electron transfer and promising electron reservoir properties of the CDs [24–28]. Interestingly, antibacterial activity was still observed after storage of the CDs/Na2W4O13/WO3 composite in the dark for 24 h, with only 20.9% live E. coli cells remaining after treatment for 100 min in the dark. Several previous studies demonstrated that CDs exhibit antibacterial activity against both gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria [41,42]. Thus, the bactericidal activity of the CDs/Na2W4O13/WO3 composite under dark conditions was possibly due to the antibacterial effect of the CDs [41,42].

SEM observation could directly reveal morphology changes of E. coli cells treated by the CDs/Na2W4O13/WO3 composite. Fig. 6 a showed the SEM image of E. coli cells illuminated by visible light for 100 min without the presence of CDs/Na2W4O13/WO3 composites. In this control experiment sample, the E. coli cells have smooth surface, indicated their damage-free and healthy status. Fig. 6b showed the SEM image of E. coli cells after being illuminated by visible light for 100 min with the presence of CDs/Na2W4O13/WO3 composites. Although the cells still kept their basic shape, obvious shrank could be clearly observed. This may be due to the leakage of the cellular inclusions from the damaged cell wall/membrane. In previous works, it has demonstrated that the ROS produced in photocatalysis progress induced cell wall/cell membrane damaged and even cell broken [43].

Generally, various ROS, such as hydroxyl radicals (OH), superoxide radicals (O2 −), and photogenerated electrons (e−) and holes (h+), may give rise to photocatalytic bacterial inactivation. To understand which of these species played the most important role in the photocatalytic bacterial inactivation demonstrated by the CDs/Na2W4O13/WO3 composite, four different scavengers, potassium dichromate, sodium oxalate, isopropyl alcohol and 4-hydroxy-TEMPO, were chosen to study the presence of e−, h+, OH and O2 −, respectively, based on the method recommended in a previous report [44]. As shown in Fig. 7 , the scavengers caused a decrease in the reaction rate in the order isopropyl alcohol >4-hydroxy-TEMPO > sodium oxalate > Cr (VI). The bacterial inactivation efficiency was significantly inhibited by the addition of isopropyl alcohol and 4-hydroxy-TEMPO to quench the OH and O2 − species, indicating that they played a critical role and made a large contribution in the photocatalytic inactivation process. Partial inhibition of the inactivation efficiency was observed following the addition of sodium oxalate to the reaction mixture. However, the bacterial inactivation was decreased about two-log reduction when Cr (VI) was added, suggesting that photogenerated electrons had a minor contribution of the direct inactivation process. The involvement of OH and O2 − in the photocatalytic inactivation process of the CDs/Na2W4O13/WO3 composite could be due to the fast electron transfer rate. Note that the OH and O2 − ROS may be not the main active species generated in the photocatalysis process of pure WO3 under visible light irradiation [32].

To further examine the bacterial inactivation mechanism in the photoreaction process, the existence of OH and O2 − in the CDs/Na2W4O13/WO3 composite photocatalytic system was further confirmed by using a DMPO spin-trapping ESR technique. As shown in Fig. 8 , no obvious signals were detected in either ESR spectrum in the dark. However, the signals of both DMPO-O2 − and DMPO-OH were clearly observed under visible light illumination. Moreover, the signals of DMPO-O2 − and DMPO-•OH gradually increased with increasing irradiation time. These results confirmed that both OH and O2 − were produced in the CDs/Na2W4O13/WO3 system, and these species ultimately contributed to the photocatalytic disinfection activity of the CDs/Na2W4O13/WO3 composite.

As a micrometer grade two-dimensional material, the CDs/Na2W4O13/WO3 composite should not pose threat to the personnel performing its application. MTT and CCK-8 assays are commonly used for in vitro cytotoxicity testing. However, the CCK-8 assay is more sensitive than the MTT assay because it reﬂects the overall cell viability, while the MTT assay shows the mitochondrial activity of the cells [45]. In this paper, the cytotoxicity of the as-synthesized composite was tested by using CCK-8 assays to evaluate the viability of HepG2 and V79 cells. The CCK-8-based cytotoxicity test showed that the viability of the HepG2 and V79 cells was not lost after 24 or 48 h of incubation in the dark with 0.1 mg/mL, 0.2 mg/mL, 0.4 mg/mL and 0.8 mg/mL of the CDs/Na2W4O13/WO3 composite, and all of the treated cells were alive (see in Fig. 9 ).Thus, the CDs/Na2W4O13/WO3 composite is non-toxic and could be used as an environmentally friendly material. Light microscopy images of untreated HepG2 andV79 cells and of those exposed to 0.8 mg/mL of the CDs/Na2W4O13/WO3 composite for different time intervals are shown in Fig. S5. There were no obvious differences in the morphologies of the HepG2 and V79 cells between treated and untreated cells and between the different treatment durations.

In conclusion, a CDs/Na2W4O13/WO3 composite photocatalyst was successfully synthesized by a novel method of facile hydrothermal-calcination without the addition of previously prepared CDs. The characterization results confirmed that CD nanoparticles decorated the surface of Na2W4O13. Compared to Na2W4O13, WO3 and Na2W4O13/WO3, the CDs/Na2W4O13/WO3 composite displayed enhanced photocatalytic disinfection of E. coli cells under visible light irradiation. This enhanced photocatalytic disinfection performance was attributed to the photocatalytic production of both OH and O2 − by the CDs/Na2W4O13/WO3 composite, which was confirmed by scavenger and ESR experiments. In addition, the cytotoxicity assessment with HepG2 and V79 cells showed that the composite was non-toxic. Hence, our results demonstrated that the CDs/Na2W4O13/WO3 composite has great potential for use as an environmentally friendly antibacterial material.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.
The manuscript was written through contributions of all authors. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful for the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21571160), the National Natural Science Foundation of China-Henan Talents Fostering Joint Funds (No. U1504311), and the Key Research Projects of the Science and Technology Department of Henan Province (No. 152102210147 and No. 172102210544) for funding. We thank Professor Yawei Han, Doctor Wenshan Zhou and Miss Panpan Li for their assistance in the cell culture and CCK assay.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhazmat.2018.06.072.
Details of the reagents, TEM image, SEM image and XPS spectrum of synthesized composite, microscopy images of the V79 cell and HepG2 cell.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Antibacterial resistance worldwide: causes, challenges and responses
The challenge of overcoming antibiotic resistance: an adjuvant approach
Negligible particle-specific antibacterial activity of silver nanoparticles
Photocatalytic materials and technologies for air purification
Antimicrobial nanomaterials for water disinfection and microbial control: potential applications and implications
